title: ACC SRVY 2344 — Surveying Legal Principles II

lessons.title: Week 1: Review of Legal Principles I and Advanced Deed Analysis
learning_objectives: Recall the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine as the controlling framework for retracement.
Apply intent-of-the-parties and four-corners construction to ambiguous and conflicting deed language.
Analyze a chain of title across multiple instruments to identify senior rights, gaps, and defective links.
key_takeaways: Intent of the parties, gathered from the four corners read in light of surrounding circumstances, is the paramount rule of deed construction.
The dignity-of-calls hierarchy is only a rule of construction; it yields to expressed intent and to the original surveyor's footsteps.
Advanced deed analysis means harmonizing every call across the whole instrument and the whole chain, not reading any single call in isolation.

content.html:
From Legal Principles I to II

This second course assumes you already command the foundations from SRVY 2343: estates and deeds, the recording act, easements, adverse possession, and the basic dignity-of-calls hierarchy. Legal Principles II is the advanced retracement course — it returns to those doctrines under harder facts: deeds that conflict with one another, conveyances created out of order or all at once, water boundaries that move, severed mineral estates, and the surveyor as an expert witness. Week 1 re-grounds you in the two ideas that govern everything that follows. First, the intent of the parties, gathered from the four corners of the instrument read in light of the surrounding circumstances, is the paramount objective of construction. Second, when calls within a description conflict, Texas ranks them by relative reliability (the dignity of calls), but that ranking is only a tool for finding intent — it always yields to the controlling intent and to the footsteps of the original surveyor on the ground.

rows: Most reliable; hard to move or mistake
Artificial monuments (set iron, stakes, marked trees, fences)
Reliable if found and identified as original
Modern Texas refinement, between artificial monuments and course
Yields to course and to monuments
Weakest; a check, not a control

content.text: Texas differs: the dignity-of-calls hierarchy is a rebuttable rule of construction, not a mechanical formula. Per Stafford v. King, a found and identified original monument controls over conflicting field-note calls, and a lower-dignity call can prevail when the instrument and circumstances show that was the intent.

sections.title: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867) — the anchor case
sections.content: The foundational Texas authority on both the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine. The Court explained that natural objects (mountains, lakes, rivers, creeks, rocks) outrank artificial objects (marked lines, trees, stakes), and that course and distance are the most unreliable calls and generally yield to monuments. It summarized the controlling order as natural objects, then artificial objects, then course and distance. Critically, the construction adopted must be the one most consistent with the intention apparent on the face of the patent, read in light of surrounding circumstances. The case also states the footsteps rule: 'The actual identification of the survey, the footsteps of the surveyor upon the ground, should always be followed, by whatever rule they may be traced.'

sections.title: Bass v. Harper, 441 S.W.2d 825 (Tex. 1969) — harmonize the whole instrument
sections.content: Modern deed construction focuses on the intent expressed within the deed and on harmonizing all of its parts so that no clause is rendered meaningless if it can be avoided. When two descriptions in the same instrument conflict, the court tries first to reconcile them; only when they cannot be harmonized does the priority-of-calls or the specific-controls-general canon resolve the conflict.

content.html:
What 'advanced' deed analysis adds

Basic analysis reads one deed and applies the hierarchy. Advanced analysis reads the whole chain and asks how several instruments interact. You will: (1) trace title back toward the original GLO survey and abstract to find the senior grant; (2) compare the calls of adjoining deeds to detect overlaps (the same ground described twice) and gaps/hiatus (ground described by neither); (3) classify each conveyance as senior or junior and as sequential or simultaneous, because that classification dictates whether excess/deficiency is given to the junior or apportioned; and (4) apply the specific-controls-general rule — a metes-and-bounds description controls over a general or quantity description when they cannot be harmonized (Concho Resources v. Ellison, Tex. 2021). The surveyor does not decide title, but must marshal and weigh this evidence so the boundary opinion reflects the legal framework a court would apply.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Construe for intent from the four corners; use the dignity of calls only to resolve genuine conflicts.
Found, identified original monuments and the surveyor's footsteps outrank conflicting course/distance/quantity calls.
When a specific metes-and-bounds description conflicts irreconcilably with a general one, the specific controls (Concho v. Ellison).
Advanced analysis works across the whole chain to expose overlaps, gaps, and senior/junior relationships.

flashcards.definition: The Texas reliability hierarchy for conflicting calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity. A rule of construction that yields to intent and to the original surveyor's footsteps.
flashcards.hint_1: Most reliable thing on the ground wins.

flashcards.definition: The cardinal Texas retracement rule from Stafford v. King: the retracing surveyor must follow the actual footsteps of the original surveyor on the ground, even against later, more 'accurate' field-note calls.
flashcards.hint_1: Where the line was run, not where it should have been.

flashcards.definition: Construction canon (Concho Resources v. Ellison, Tex. 2021): when a specific metes-and-bounds description and a general/quantity description cannot be harmonized, the specific description governs.
flashcards.hint_1: Detailed calls beat a vague acreage recital.

lessons.title: Week 2: Simultaneous and Sequential Conveyances
learning_objectives: Distinguish sequential from simultaneous conveyances and identify each in a real chain of title.
Apply senior/junior rights to resolve overlaps and deficiencies in sequential conveyances.
Apply proportionate apportionment to distribute excess or deficiency among simultaneously created lots.
key_takeaways: Sequential conveyances are governed by senior/junior rights; the senior takes its full description and the junior absorbs any excess or deficiency.
Simultaneous conveyances (a recorded subdivision plat) carry no seniority; excess/deficiency is apportioned among lots in proportion to record dimensions.
The order in which subdivision lots later sell is immaterial to seniority among them.

content.html:
Why creation order controls the math

The single most important question before resolving a boundary excess or deficiency is: were these parcels created sequentially or simultaneously? The answer determines whether one parcel absorbs the entire discrepancy or whether the discrepancy is spread proportionately. Sequential conveyances are carved out of a larger tract one after another over time — the classic metes-and-bounds carve-out. Simultaneous conveyances are created at one instant from a common scheme — classically, the lots shown on a recorded subdivision plat. Both patterns are everyday Texas facts: a rancher selling off tracts piece by piece is creating sequential conveyances; a developer recording a plat of 40 lots is creating simultaneous ones.

rows: Junior absorbs it; senior made whole
Spread among lots by record proportion

content.html:
Senior/junior rights in sequential conveyances

A grantor cannot convey land already conveyed. When parcels are carved out over time, the earlier (senior) conveyance takes the full measure of what its description grants; the later (junior) conveyance takes only the remainder. Seniority resolves both directions of error: if the ground turns out short, the junior eats the deficiency so the senior still gets its full described width; if the ground is long, the junior takes the surplus. Seniority does not expand a grant beyond its own description — it only allocates conflict in the senior's favor. Note the Texas regulatory overlay: TBPELS standards in 22 TAC Ch. 138 affirmatively direct surveyors to respect junior/senior rights and the record chain during retracement.

Apportionment in simultaneous conveyances

Because no platted lot is senior to its neighbors, an excess or deficiency found between controlling, undisputed monuments at the block ends is apportioned proportionately among the interior lots according to their record lengths — no single lot absorbs the whole error. This is the surveyor's everyday block-breakdown problem. Two cautions: apportionment is a last resort, applied only after found original monuments and corners control first and only where a corner is genuinely lost (not merely obliterated and recoverable from collateral evidence); and apportionment distributes ordinary measurement error, not an isolable gross blunder, which is placed where it belongs rather than smeared across the block.

content.text: Texas note: the single/double proportionate-measurement methods are PLSS/BLM-Manual constructs. No Texas Supreme Court opinion adopts them by name. In Texas, apportionment is common-law surveying practice and a genuine fallback behind found monuments and the original surveyor's footsteps — because most Texas land sits on GLO original surveys, not the federal township-range grid.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Classify creation order first: sequential (senior/junior) versus simultaneous (apportion).
Senior takes its full description; the junior absorbs surplus or deficiency.
Apportion plat excess/deficiency among lots by record proportion — but only for lost corners after found monuments control.
Never apportion a gross blunder; place it where the evidence shows it belongs.

flashcards.definition: Multiple parcels created at one instant from a common scheme (classically, lots on a recorded subdivision plat). No lot is senior; excess/deficiency is apportioned among lots by record proportion.
flashcards.hint_1: All lots born at the same moment.

flashcards.definition: In sequential conveyances, the earlier grant takes the full measure of its description; the later (junior) grant takes only the remainder and absorbs any excess or deficiency.
flashcards.hint_1: First conveyed, first served.

flashcards.definition: Distributing a measured excess or deficiency between controlling monuments among simultaneously created lots in proportion to their record lengths, used only as a last resort after found monuments control.
flashcards.hint_1: Spread the slop by record share.

lessons.title: Week 3: Advanced Boundary Conflict Resolution
learning_objectives: Classify boundary conflicts as overlaps, gaps/hiatus, or vacancies and select the correct resolution path.
Quantify the width of a gap or overlap from adjoining record calls.
Explain single and double proportionate measurement and when each restores a lost corner.
key_takeaways: An overlap is ground claimed twice; a gap/hiatus is ground claimed by neither; resolution turns on seniority and the original footsteps.
Single proportion restores a lost corner on one line between two monuments; double proportion restores a corner controlled in two directions.
Found monuments and ascertainable footsteps always outrank proportioning.

content.html:
The three classic conflicts

Advanced retracement constantly produces three structural conflicts. An overlap occurs when two descriptions cover the same ground — the senior grant prevails and the junior is cut back to the residue. A gap (also called a hiatus) occurs when adjoining descriptions leave a strip claimed by neither; the strip belongs to whoever the record and footsteps show it belongs to, and it cannot be created or destroyed merely by re-measuring. A vacancy is a special Texas concept: unsurveyed, unappropriated land between or within original GLO surveys that was never severed from the State — title to a true vacancy remains in the sovereign and is administered by the GLO, not the adjoining private owners. Distinguishing a private gap from a State vacancy is a serious title question, not a math exercise.

rows: Same ground described in two deeds
Senior prevails; junior cut to remainder
Strip described by neither adjoining deed
Belongs per record + footsteps; cannot be re-measured away
Unappropriated land never severed from the State
Title in the State; GLO administers
Original corner position cannot be recovered from evidence
Original gone but position recoverable from collateral evidence
Re-establish from evidence; do NOT proportion

sections.title: Single proportionate measurement (two-way)
sections.content: Restores a lost corner that lies on a single line between two found, undisputed monuments. The corrected interval bears the same ratio to the corrected whole as the record interval bears to the record whole. If the record distance from monument A to the lost corner is d, the record whole A-to-B is D_rec, and the measured whole is D_meas, then the restored distance is d × (D_meas / D_rec). This is the everyday block-breakdown and interior-lot computation.

sections.title: Double proportionate measurement
sections.content: Restores a lost corner controlled in two directions at once — classically a corner common to four sections, re-established from four surrounding found monuments by proportioning independently along the latitudinal and the longitudinal lines. The two independent proportioned positions fix the restored corner. Double proportion is a PLSS/BLM-Manual construct used where a corner is genuinely lost in both directions.

sections.title: Obliterated versus lost — the threshold question
sections.content: Proportioning is a last resort. A corner is obliterated (not lost) if its original position can be recovered from collateral evidence — testimony, fences built to it, occupation lines, references in adjoining deeds. An obliterated corner is re-established from that evidence and must NOT be proportioned. Only a genuinely lost corner — no recoverable evidence — is restored by proportionate measurement.

content.text: Texas note: because Texas land flows from GLO original surveys rather than the federal PLSS grid, an apparent 'gap' between surveys may be a State vacancy. Do not resolve it as a private gap or proration without analyzing whether the strip was ever severed from the sovereign and whether the GLO must be involved.

content.html:
Choosing the path

Resolution discipline runs in a fixed order. First, follow the footsteps — locate the original surveyor's actual lines and corners; if found, they control. Second, honor found monuments by dignity of calls. Third, apply senior/junior rights for overlaps in sequential conveyances. Fourth, apportion only for lost corners in simultaneous conveyances. Throughout, keep the gap/vacancy distinction in mind, and remember a gross blunder is isolated and placed, never smeared by proration. The surveyor documents which path was taken and why, because that reasoning is the heart of the boundary opinion a court will scrutinize.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Overlap = senior wins; gap = belongs per record/footsteps; vacancy = State land via GLO.
Single proportion fixes a lost corner on one line; double proportion fixes one controlled in two directions.
Obliterated corners are re-established from evidence and never proportioned.
Footsteps and found monuments always precede proportioning; a true Texas gap may be a State vacancy.

flashcards.definition: A strip of ground left between adjoining descriptions that is called for by neither. Its ownership is fixed by the record and the original footsteps and cannot be created or erased by re-measuring.
flashcards.hint_1: Land nobody's deed reaches.

flashcards.definition: Unsurveyed, unappropriated land never severed from the sovereign that lies between or within original GLO surveys. Title remains in the State and is administered by the GLO.
flashcards.hint_1: Still the State's, never patented.

flashcards.definition: An obliterated corner's position is recoverable from collateral evidence and is re-established from it (no proration). A lost corner has no recoverable evidence and is restored by proportionate measurement.
flashcards.hint_1: Evidence exists = obliterated; none = lost.

lessons.title: Week 4: Complex Riparian and Littoral Boundaries
learning_objectives: Apply the Texas gradient boundary method and the 30-foot navigability rule to navigable streams.
Select the correct tidal datum (MHHW vs. MHW) based on the sovereign source of a coastal grant.
Distinguish accretion, reliction, erosion, and avulsion and explain their effect on a boundary.
key_takeaways: Texas fixes the bed of a navigable stream at the gradient boundary, midway up the qualified cut bank, not at the ordinary high-water mark.
Coastal datum depends on the sovereign source: Spanish/Mexican grants use mean higher high water; Anglo/Texas grants use mean high tide.
Gradual change (accretion/erosion) moves the boundary; sudden change (avulsion) fixes it.

content.html:
Texas water boundaries are genuinely unusual

Texas water-boundary law diverges sharply from general practice, so before choosing any rule you must establish two facts: is the water navigable, and what sovereign issued the grant. A stream is navigable in fact when it 'retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up' (Tex. Nat. Res. Code §21.001(3)) — a bright-line geometric test measured across the entire bed, so a sometimes-dry channel can still be navigable. The State owns the beds of navigable streams in trust for the public; private patents stop at the bed margin, and the abutting owner of a non-navigable stream takes to the center thread of the channel.

sections.title: Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926) — the gradient boundary
sections.content: The Texas Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary as the line dividing State-owned bed from private upland along a navigable stream. The boundary is a graded line along the bank, located midway between the lower water level that just reaches the cut bank and the higher level that just does not overtop it. The Court defined the 'bed' as soil alternately covered and left bare, adequate to contain the water at its average and mean annual stage, disregarding extreme freshets and droughts. This is a difficult expert survey — water-level observation, bank profiling, and gradient interpolation between qualified banks.

sections.title: Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000) — modern refinement
sections.content: The leading modern application (Canadian River) restated and refined the gradient-boundary location method as the mid-height point of the lowest acceptable or 'qualified' bank in the vicinity. It confirms that the gradient boundary remains the controlling Texas method and clarifies how to choose the qualified bank when the actual bank is irregular.

sections.title: Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959) — the dual tidal datum
sections.content: The seaward boundary of coastal upland depends on the sovereign source. A Spanish/Mexican grant is bounded by mean higher high water (MHHW) — the average of the higher of the two daily high tides over an ~18.6-year epoch, which pushes the line landward and enlarges the State's seabed. An Anglo-American / Republic- or State-of-Texas grant is bounded by mean high tide / mean high water (the average of all high tides). The difference flows from Spanish-Mexican civil law defining the shore by the reach of the highest winter tides.

content.text: Texas differs: virtually every other state fixes a riparian boundary at the ordinary high-water mark and a coastal boundary at mean high tide universally. Texas uses a computed gradient line on navigable streams and a DUAL coastal datum (MHHW for civil-law grants, MHW for Anglo grants). Always trace the chain back to the original sovereign before selecting a datum.

rows: Boundary moves landward; owner loses land

content.html:
Movement, the Open Beaches Act, and curative statutes

The gradual-versus-sudden rule controls movement: accretion, reliction, and erosion move the boundary; avulsion fixes it at the prior location. On the Gulf, the Open Beaches Act (Tex. Nat. Res. Code Ch. 61) guarantees public use of the dry beach between mean low tide and the vegetation line under a public easement, historically 'rolling' landward as the vegetation line migrates. In Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012), the Court limited that doctrine: a beach easement may migrate gradually but does not automatically roll onto previously private dry land after an avulsive event — after avulsion the State must establish a new easement. Finally, the 1929 'Small Bill' relinquished the State's interest in certain streambed surfaces to adjoining owners while reserving minerals to the State and preserving public water rights — so an owner can hold the bed surface yet the public keeps navigation rights and the State keeps the minerals.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Navigability test: average bed width of 30 feet (Nat. Res. Code 21.001(3)); State owns navigable beds.
Navigable-stream boundary = gradient boundary, midway up the qualified cut bank (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard).
Coastal datum follows the sovereign: MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Anglo/Texas grants (Luttes).
Accretion/erosion move the line; avulsion fixes it (Severance limits the rolling-easement rule after avulsion).

flashcards.definition: The Texas line dividing State-owned bed from private upland on a navigable stream: a graded line midway between the water level that just reaches the cut bank and the level that just fails to overtop it (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard).
flashcards.hint_1: Mid-height of the qualified cut bank.

flashcards.definition: Texas coastal boundary rule: Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by mean higher high water (MHHW); Anglo-American/Texas grants by mean high tide (MHW). The sovereign source picks the datum.
flashcards.hint_1: Civil-law grant = the higher, landward line.

flashcards.definition: A sudden, perceptible change in a watercourse (flood cutting a new channel, a hurricane). The boundary is FIXED at its former location and does not follow the new water line.
flashcards.hint_1: Sudden = boundary frozen.

lessons.title: Week 5: Mineral Rights and the Severed Estate
learning_objectives: Explain how a mineral estate is severed from the surface and which estate is dominant in Texas.
Describe the surface owner's protections and the surveyor's role in documenting severed estates.
Identify State mineral reservations arising from the Small Bill and Relinquishment Act lands.
key_takeaways: Texas treats the severed mineral estate as dominant, with an implied right to use the surface reasonably to develop minerals.
Severance creates two estates from one tract; a surveyor must show easements, reservations, and access affecting the surface.
The 1929 Small Bill reserved streambed minerals to the State even where the bed surface was relinquished.

content.html:
Two estates from one tract

A single tract of land contains a surface estate and a mineral estate. When the minerals are conveyed or reserved separately — a severance — the two estates can have entirely different owners, chains of title, and rights. In Texas this is the norm rather than the exception, and it profoundly affects a surveyor's work: the boundary you locate on the ground describes the surface, but the records may show a severed mineral estate, oil-and-gas leases, pooling, and access easements that burden that surface. A complete boundary product accounts for these even though the surveyor does not adjudicate mineral title.

The dominant mineral estate

Texas law treats the mineral estate as dominant over the surface estate. The mineral owner (or lessee) holds an implied right to make reasonable use of the surface as necessary to explore for, develop, and produce the minerals — roads, pads, pipelines, and the like — without paying the surface owner for that ordinary use, unless a lease or deed says otherwise. This dominance is bounded: the use must be reasonably necessary, and the accommodation doctrine requires the mineral developer to accommodate an existing surface use where reasonable alternatives exist. For the surveyor, the practical consequence is that surface improvements, access roads, and well sites may lawfully exist that the surface deed never mentions; they must be observed, located, and tied to the record where possible.

content.text: Texas note: the mineral estate is the DOMINANT estate and carries an implied surface easement to develop minerals. A surface boundary survey does not resolve mineral ownership, but the surveyor should flag visible mineral-development features and disclose that the survey addresses the surface estate only unless the scope says otherwise.

sections.title: The Small Bill (1929) — State streambed minerals
sections.content: Codified historically as Art. 5414a, Vernon's Revised Civil Statutes, the 1929 Small Bill relinquished the State's interest in certain streambed surfaces to adjoining patentees to cure title clouds where earlier surveys crossed streambeds. Critically it reserved the minerals (and sand/gravel) to the State and preserved public water rights. So along a navigable Texas stream the adjoining owner may hold the bed surface, yet the State still owns the minerals beneath it.

sections.title: Relinquishment Act lands
sections.content: On certain classified state lands, the surface owner acts as the State's agent for leasing the minerals, sharing the proceeds with the Permanent School Fund, while the State retains the mineral ownership. The exact mineral-classified-land cutoff date varies across sources and should be verified against the statute before relying on it in a title context. The teaching point for surveyors is that 'fee' ownership of the surface does not always carry the minerals, especially on lands traceable to State classification.

content.html:
The surveyor's role with severed estates

The surveyor's job is to make the surface boundary unambiguous and to disclose the record encumbrances that touch the surface: mineral reservations, lease and pipeline easements, surface-use agreements, and access roads. Where the scope is a surface boundary survey, state clearly that mineral title is not addressed. Where features on the ground (tank batteries, pad sites, gathering lines) evidence mineral development, locate and label them, and tie them to recorded instruments when the records permit. This protects both the client and the surveyor by ensuring the boundary product reflects the real, multi-estate condition of the land.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Severance splits surface and mineral estates into separate ownerships and chains of title.
The mineral estate is dominant in Texas, with an implied right of reasonable surface use, tempered by the accommodation doctrine.
The Small Bill reserved streambed minerals to the State even where bed surface was relinquished.
A surface boundary survey should disclose mineral reservations and locate visible development features, without adjudicating mineral title.

flashcards.definition: A mineral estate conveyed or reserved separately from the surface, creating two estates with different owners and chains of title from one original tract.
flashcards.hint_1: Minerals split off from the surface.

flashcards.definition: The Texas rule that the mineral estate prevails over the surface, carrying an implied right to make reasonable use of the surface to develop minerals, limited by the accommodation doctrine.
flashcards.hint_1: Minerals get to use the surface to produce.

flashcards.definition: Under the 1929 Small Bill (Art. 5414a), the State relinquished certain streambed surfaces to adjoining owners but reserved the minerals (and sand/gravel) and preserved public water rights.
flashcards.hint_1: You can own the bed surface; the State keeps what's under it.

lessons.title: Week 6: Eminent Domain and Condemnation Surveys
learning_objectives: Define eminent domain and condemnation and the constitutional requirements of public use and just compensation.
Describe the parts/whole-take survey deliverables and the parent-tract analysis used to value a partial taking.
Explain the surveyor's role and exposure in preparing right-of-way and condemnation descriptions.
key_takeaways: Eminent domain requires a public use and adequate (just) compensation under the Texas Constitution.
A partial taking requires a parent-tract analysis: the part taken, the remainder, and damages/benefits to the remainder.
Condemnation descriptions demand high precision because they fix what the public acquires and what the owner is paid for.

content.html:
Eminent domain and condemnation

Eminent domain is the sovereign power to take private property for public use; condemnation is the legal proceeding that exercises that power. In Texas the power is constrained by Tex. Const. art. I, §17, which forbids taking, damaging, or destroying private property for public use without adequate compensation and (after the 2009 amendment) confines takings to genuine public uses, generally barring takings transferred to private parties for economic-development purposes. The condemnor — a governmental body or an entity with delegated power, such as a utility or pipeline — must establish public use and pay just compensation, typically determined first by special commissioners and then, on objection, by a court.

Whole takes, partial takes, and the parent tract

A whole take acquires the entire parcel; compensation is the market value of the whole. The harder problem is the partial take, where the project acquires a strip or corner — a right-of-way for a road, pipeline, or transmission line. Valuing a partial take requires a parent-tract analysis: identify the larger parcel as it existed before the taking, then quantify (1) the value of the part taken, and (2) damages to the remainder (severance damages), offset by any special benefits the project confers on the remainder. The surveyor supplies the geometric backbone of this analysis: the parent-tract boundary, the take area, the remainder areas, and any uneconomic remnants the take creates.

rows: The whole as it existed before the take
Baseline for valuation and remainder analysis
Defines exactly what the public acquires
Area and shape left to the owner
Take across many ownerships along a corridor
Whether fee title or an easement is taken

content.text: Texas note: art. I, sec. 17 requires ADEQUATE compensation for property taken, damaged, or destroyed for public use, and the 2009 constitutional amendment restricts takings for transfer to private parties for economic development. Right-of-way and condemnation descriptions must be drafted to that standard of precision and reviewability.

content.html:
The surveyor's role and exposure

Condemnation work is unforgiving because the description fixes both what the public acquires and what the owner is paid for. The surveyor must: tie the take to the parent-tract boundary and to controlling monuments; distinguish a fee acquisition from an easement acquisition (the latter leaves the underlying fee and residual rights in the owner); compute take and remainder areas accurately; and identify uneconomic remnants — slivers the take renders unusable — which can drive damages. Because these descriptions are litigated and become the operative legal instruments, they must meet the technical standards of 22 TAC Ch. 138 and withstand cross-examination. Errors here translate directly into over- or under-compensation and professional liability.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Eminent domain requires public use and adequate compensation (Tex. Const. art. I, sec. 17).
Partial takes need a parent-tract analysis: part taken plus damages to the remainder, offset by special benefits.
Distinguish fee acquisitions from easement acquisitions; they leave different residual rights.
Condemnation descriptions are litigated legal instruments demanding maximum precision and reviewability.

flashcards.definition: The sovereign power to take private property for public use, exercised through condemnation and constrained in Texas by art. I, sec. 17's requirements of public use and adequate compensation.
flashcards.hint_1: Government takes; condemnation is the process.

flashcards.definition: The framework for valuing a partial taking: identify the larger parcel before the take, then value the part taken plus severance damages to the remainder, offset by special benefits.
flashcards.hint_1: Whole before, part taken, remainder after.

flashcards.definition: A portion of the remainder so small or oddly shaped after a partial taking that it has little or no independent utility; its impaired value increases just compensation.
flashcards.hint_1: The leftover sliver no one can use.

lessons.title: Week 7: Subdivision Platting Law and Municipal Regulations
learning_objectives: Identify when a Texas plat is required and the statutory approval and recording chain.
Distinguish statutory, common-law, and implied dedication and the 1981 limitation on implied dedication.
Explain the surveyor's responsibilities in preparing and certifying a subdivision plat.
key_takeaways: Dividing a tract into a subdivision (including by metes-and-bounds) within a municipality or its ETJ triggers the plat requirement (Local Gov't Code 212.004).
A plat may not be recorded without required approval and a tax certificate (Property Code 12.002).
In counties of 50,000 or fewer, implied dedication of a public road has been abolished since August 1981 (Transp. Code Ch. 281).

content.html:
When a plat is required

Texas regulates the division of land through platting. Under Local Government Code §212.004 ('Plat Required'), an owner who divides a tract within a municipality or its extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) into two or more parts to lay out a subdivision, lots, streets, alleys, or parks must have a plat prepared. The statute is explicit that a division 'includes a division … made by using a metes and bounds description in a deed of conveyance or in a contract for deed … or by using any other method' — closing the loophole of subdividing informally by deed. A common exception covers divisions into parts greater than five acres where each has access and no public improvement is dedicated.

rows: Subdivision within city or ETJ must be platted
Filed and recorded with the county clerk

content.html:
Recording prerequisites

Approval is not recording. Under Property Code §12.002 ('Subdivision Plat; Penalty'), a county clerk may not record a plat or replat unless it is approved as required by law by the appropriate authority and is accompanied by the documents the Local Government Code requires — including an original tax certificate from each taxing unit showing no delinquent ad valorem taxes. The plat must be acknowledged like a deed. A standalone metes-and-bounds conveyance of a larger rural tract that does not create a subdivision records under Property Code Ch. 12 without a plat; it is the act of subdividing within a regulated area that triggers the Ch. 212 / Ch. 232 plat-approval-recording chain.

sections.title: Three kinds of dedication
sections.content: Statutory dedication is made by recording a plat that marks streets and public areas under the platting statutes. Common-law dedication requires the owner's donative intent, public reliance or benefit, and offer plus acceptance (formal or informal — the authority grades or repairs the road, the public uses it, the county stops taxing it). Implied dedication historically arose from a course of conduct plus public use with no writing, needing the three elements plus an additional factor implying donative intent. Plat approval alone is not automatic acceptance; some affirmative public acceptance is generally still needed.

sections.title: The 1981 limitation — Transp. Code Ch. 281
sections.content: Effective August 1981, the Legislature abolished implied dedication as a means of establishing a public road on private property in counties of 50,000 or fewer. There, a public interest in a private road can be acquired only by purchase, condemnation, an express written dedication to the commissioners court, or a final judgment of adverse possession. Oral or 'overt act' implied dedication no longer suffices in covered counties. The change is not retroactive — roads impliedly dedicated before August 1981 can still be established under the old doctrine.

content.text: Texas differs: under general common law, implied dedication of public roads remains viable. In Texas counties of 50,000 or fewer, implied dedication has been abolished since August 1981 (Transp. Code Ch. 281). For a post-1981 road in a small county, public status cannot rest on 'everyone has always used it' — you need a written dedication, purchase, condemnation, or an adverse-possession judgment.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Subdividing within a city or ETJ requires a plat, even when done by metes-and-bounds (Local Gov't Code 212.004).
A plat cannot be recorded without proper approval and a tax certificate (Property Code 12.002).
Dedication comes in statutory, common-law, and implied forms; plat approval is not automatic acceptance.
Implied dedication of public roads is abolished in counties of 50,000 or fewer since August 1981 (Transp. Code Ch. 281).

flashcards.definition: Local Government Code 212.004: an owner dividing a tract within a municipality or its ETJ into a subdivision must prepare a plat, including divisions made by metes-and-bounds description.
flashcards.hint_1: Can't subdivide a city tract by deed alone.

flashcards.definition: Transp. Code Ch. 281 (eff. Aug. 1981): in counties of 50,000 or fewer, a public road cannot be created by implied/oral dedication; it requires written dedication, purchase, condemnation, or an adverse-possession judgment.
flashcards.hint_1: Small counties need it in writing now.

flashcards.definition: Property Code 12.002: a county clerk may not record a plat without required governmental approval and a tax certificate showing no delinquent ad valorem taxes; the plat must be acknowledged.
flashcards.hint_1: Approval plus paid taxes before filing.

lessons.title: Week 8: Midterm Exam
learning_objectives: Integrate Weeks 1-7 doctrines to resolve multi-issue boundary problems.
Select the correct conflict-resolution path under realistic chain-of-title facts.
Justify boundary conclusions with Texas authority and the dignity-of-calls/footsteps framework.
key_takeaways: The midterm rewards spotting which doctrine controls before computing anything.
Most problems hinge on creation order (senior/junior vs. apportionment) and on found monuments/footsteps.
Water and severed-estate questions turn on sovereign source and navigability.

content.html:
Scope and strategy

The midterm covers Weeks 1 through 7: advanced deed analysis; simultaneous versus sequential conveyances; overlaps, gaps, vacancies, and proportionate measurement; complex riparian and littoral boundaries; the severed mineral estate; eminent domain and condemnation; and subdivision platting and dedication. Most questions are issue-spotting: before you compute, identify which doctrine controls. Ask in order — what is the intent of the instrument; are the parcels sequential or simultaneous; are there found monuments or ascertainable footsteps; is the water navigable and from what sovereign did the grant issue; is a mineral or public interest in play. Naming the controlling rule is usually worth more than the arithmetic.

rows: Lots on a recorded plat, total measures long/short
Tracts carved from a ranch over years, overlap
Strip claimed by neither deed between GLO surveys
Coastal line on an 1829 Mexican grant
Pipeline acquires a 50-ft strip across a farm
Developer divides a tract in the city ETJ

content.text: Exam tip: write the controlling Texas authority next to each conclusion (Stafford, Motl v. Boyd, Luttes, Tran v. Macha, Severance, 212.004). Pairing a rule with a case earns credit even when a computed number is slightly off.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Spot the doctrine before doing the math.
Creation order and found monuments/footsteps decide most boundary conflicts.
Tie every conclusion to a Texas authority by name.

flashcards.definition: The discipline of first identifying which doctrine controls a fact pattern (intent, creation order, monuments/footsteps, navigability, sovereign source) before performing any computation.
flashcards.hint_1: Name the rule before you reach for the calculator.

flashcards.definition: The fact that determines whether senior/junior rights or proportionate apportionment applies: parcels carved over time are sequential; lots created at once on a plat are simultaneous.
flashcards.hint_1: Over time vs. all at once.

lessons.title: Week 9: ALTA/NSPS Land Title Surveys — Standards and Requirements
learning_objectives: Describe the purpose of an ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey and the parties it serves.
List the core minimum standard requirements: record description, fieldwork, easements, and certification.
Relate the national ALTA/NSPS standard to the Texas Category 1A survey.
key_takeaways: An ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey is a high-standard boundary survey produced for a title insurer and lender in a commercial transaction.
It requires a record description, a current title commitment, location of easements and encroachments, and a specific certification.
Texas surveyors often perform an ALTA/NSPS survey to also satisfy the state Category 1A standard.

content.html:
What an ALTA/NSPS survey is

The ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey is a nationally standardized boundary survey jointly promulgated by the American Land Title Association and the National Society of Professional Surveyors. It exists to serve a title insurer and a lender in a commercial real-estate transaction: the survey lets the title company delete or insure over the standard 'survey exception,' and lets the lender understand exactly what secures its loan. It is a boundary survey first, but with a defined package of additional content — record analysis against a current title commitment, location of easements and encroachments, and a formal certification — performed to the published Minimum Standard Detail Requirements.

rows: Survey based on the legal description and a current title commitment
Boundary monumented and measured to the standard's relative positional precision
Plot and reference each easement and exception from the commitment (by Schedule B item)
Locate improvements; show encroachments either direction
Show abutting streets and the property's access
The prescribed certification to named parties (owner, lender, title company)
Optional, negotiated additional items (covered in Week 10)

content.html:
Title commitment and Schedule B

An ALTA/NSPS survey is performed against a current title commitment, not just the deed. The surveyor reads Schedule B — the exceptions to coverage — and plots every easement, setback, and encumbrance that is locatable, keying each plotted item to its Schedule B number, and notes those that are blanket or not locatable. This cross-reference is what lets the title company evaluate and potentially remove the survey exception. The surveyor also reports encroachments in both directions, gaps or overlaps with adjoiners, and any evidence of use (utilities, driveways) suggesting unrecorded easements. The product is a single drawing that ties the record condition to the measured ground condition.

content.text: Texas note: the ALTA/NSPS standard is national, but Texas tiers its own surveys (Category 1A being the highest land-title standard) through the TSPS Manual of Practice, which the TBPELS rules incorporate by reference. Texas surveyors commonly perform one survey to satisfy BOTH the ALTA/NSPS standard and Category 1A, and a Category 1A plat requires a separate closure report.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: An ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey serves the title insurer and lender in a commercial transaction.
It requires a current title commitment, plotted Schedule B easements, located improvements/encroachments, and a formal certification.
Texas Category 1A is the state's highest land-title standard, defined through the TSPS Manual incorporated by the TBPELS rules.
One survey can be performed to satisfy both ALTA/NSPS and Category 1A.

flashcards.definition: A nationally standardized high-standard boundary survey performed to ALTA/NSPS Minimum Standard Detail Requirements for a title insurer and lender, plotting record easements and certifying to named parties.
flashcards.hint_1: The commercial-transaction land-title survey.

flashcards.definition: The portion of a title commitment listing easements and encumbrances excepted from coverage; an ALTA/NSPS surveyor plots each locatable item and keys it to its Schedule B number.
flashcards.hint_1: The list of easements the surveyor must plot.

flashcards.definition: The highest-standard Texas boundary/land-title survey, defined by the TSPS Manual of Practice (incorporated by the TBPELS rules) and requiring a separate closure report.
flashcards.hint_1: Texas's top-tier land-title survey.

lessons.title: Week 10: ALTA/NSPS Table A Optional Items
learning_objectives: Explain the purpose of Table A and how items are selected and negotiated.
Identify common Table A items and the additional fieldwork each requires.
Describe how Table A selections drive scope, fee, and certification.
key_takeaways: Table A is a menu of optional, negotiable additional services beyond the ALTA/NSPS minimum standard.
The client, lender, and title company select Table A items in advance; each selected item expands fieldwork and fee.
Common items include monuments set, area, flood zone, zoning, utilities, and improvements.

content.html:
What Table A is

The ALTA/NSPS standard separates a mandatory core from a menu of optional items in 'Table A.' The core requirements (boundary, record analysis, easements, certification) always apply; Table A items are negotiated and selected in advance by the client, lender, and title company, and the surveyor performs only the items the parties request. Each selected item adds defined scope, fieldwork, and cost. Because Table A is contractual, the surveyor's engagement letter should list exactly which items are included, so that the eventual certification accurately reflects the work performed and the fee matches the effort.

rows: Set and describe boundary corner monuments
Compute and state the surveyed parcel area
Report setback/height/use info from a provided zoning report
Locate substantial improvements and measure key dimensions
Count and locate striped parking spaces
Show observed and/or located utility evidence and 811 markings
Survey appurtenant easements benefiting the parcel

content.text: Practice point: list every selected Table A item in the engagement letter before fieldwork. Items like utility location and offsite easements substantially expand scope and fee; certifying to an item not actually performed creates liability.

content.html:
How Table A drives scope and risk

Table A is where most of the survey's variable cost and risk live. A utilities item may require coordinating an 811 locate and depicting subsurface evidence the surveyor cannot fully verify; a zoning item relies on a third-party zoning report the surveyor reproduces but does not independently adjudicate; a flood-zone item depicts FEMA mapping that can change. The professional discipline is to (1) select items deliberately with the client and title company, (2) perform exactly what each selected item requires, and (3) qualify any item that depends on information furnished by others. The final certification should match the selected items precisely — neither claiming work not done nor omitting work performed.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Table A is an optional, negotiated menu layered on top of the mandatory ALTA/NSPS core.
Each selected item expands fieldwork, scope, fee, and certification responsibility.
Document selected items in the engagement letter and qualify items that rely on third-party information.
Certify only to the Table A items actually performed.

flashcards.definition: The menu of optional, negotiable additional services in the ALTA/NSPS standard (e.g., monuments set, area, flood zone, zoning, utilities) selected in advance by the client, lender, and title company.
flashcards.hint_1: Optional extras chosen before fieldwork.

flashcards.definition: The contractual list of which Table A items and services the survey will include, ensuring the work, fee, and final certification all match what the parties requested.
flashcards.hint_1: Put the selected items in writing first.

lessons.title: Week 11: Texas Administrative Code — TBPELS Rules for Surveyors
learning_objectives: Locate the current TBPELS surveying standards in the Texas Administrative Code.
Summarize the professional and technical standards: precision, monumentation, and description content.
Explain licensure (RPLS, LSLS) and the surveyor's standard of practice and conduct.
key_takeaways: TBPELS rules are in 22 TAC Part 6, Chs. 134/136/138; the surveying standards are in Ch. 138, Subchapter E.
Sections 138.83 (precision), 138.87 (monumentation), and 138.91 (drawing/description/report) govern the technical product.
Only an RPLS (or LSLS for state land) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description and seal a survey.

content.html:
Where the rules live in 2026

After HB 1523 (86th Leg., 2019) merged the separate engineering and surveying boards into the Texas Board of Professional Engineers and Land Surveyors (TBPELS), the surveying standards were re-codified. The current rules are in 22 Texas Administrative Code, Part 6: Ch. 134 (licensing), Ch. 136 (firm registration), and Ch. 138 ('Compliance and Professionalism for Surveyors'). The professional and technical standards of practice sit in Ch. 138, Subchapter E (§§138.81 et seq.). The older 22 TAC Chs. 661/663 were repealed and merged — citing §663 in 2026 is outdated.

content.text: Texas update: do NOT cite 22 TAC Ch. 663. The repealed 661/663 surveying standards were merged into Ch. 138 (eff. 2021); the technical standards are now in Ch. 138, Subchapter E. Likewise, the governing statute is Occupations Code Ch. 1071 (Professional Land Surveying Practices Act).

rows: Standards 'shall meet or exceed'; applies to surveys segregating a property interest
Definite, unambiguous boundaries; found vs. placed monuments; basis of bearing

sections.title: Section 138.91 — the description/report standard
sections.content: Every description must 'provide a definite and unambiguous identification of the location of such boundaries and shall describe all monuments found or placed' (138.91(b)). Reports must 'delineate the relationship between record monuments and the location of the boundaries surveyed' (138.91(a)). Courses must be 'referenced … to an identifiable and monumented line or an established geodetic system' (138.91(c)) — a stated basis of bearing such as the Texas State Plane Coordinate System on NAD83. The surveyor must state which monuments were found and which were placed (138.91(e)).

sections.title: Monumentation — section 138.87
sections.content: Monuments set by an RPLS 'shall be set at sufficient depth to retain a stable and distinctive location and be of sufficient size to withstand the deteriorating forces of nature.' The surveyor must 'set, or leave as found, an adequate quantity of monuments of a stable and reasonably permanent nature to represent or reference the property or boundary corners,' and set monuments must be marked in a way traceable to the responsible registrant or firm.

sections.title: Licensure — RPLS and LSLS
sections.content: Under Occupations Code Ch. 1071, a Registered Professional Land Surveyor (RPLS) is licensed by TBPELS; only an RPLS (or LSLS) may prepare a metes-and-bounds description, monument boundaries, and seal/sign the survey. A Licensed State Land Surveyor (LSLS) is a higher GLO-issued credential required to survey land in which the State or Permanent School Fund has an interest, including relocating original land-grant boundaries and filing field notes in the GLO.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Current surveying standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E — not the repealed Ch. 663.
138.83 sets precision/closure; 138.87 sets monumentation; 138.91 sets description/report content and basis of bearing.
Descriptions must be definite and unambiguous and state found vs. placed monuments.
Only an RPLS may seal a survey; an LSLS is required for original state-land boundary work.

flashcards.definition: The current TBPELS professional and technical standards of practice for surveyors (sections 138.81 et seq.), replacing the repealed 22 TAC Chs. 661/663.
flashcards.hint_1: Where the surveying standards live now.

flashcards.definition: Registered Professional Land Surveyor, licensed by TBPELS under Occupations Code Ch. 1071; the only professional (besides an LSLS) who may prepare a metes-and-bounds description and seal a survey.
flashcards.hint_1: The license to sign the survey.

flashcards.definition: Licensed State Land Surveyor, a higher GLO-issued credential required to survey land in which the State or Permanent School Fund has an interest and to relocate original land-grant boundaries.
flashcards.hint_1: Needed for original state-land work.

lessons.title: Week 12: Advanced Texas Case Law Analysis
learning_objectives: Read and brief leading Texas boundary cases for their controlling holdings.
Apply Tran v. Macha, Hamrick v. Ward, Severance, and Concho v. Ellison to new facts.
Synthesize case law with statutory rules to predict how a court would locate a boundary.
key_takeaways: Texas disfavors prescriptive easements; joint use defeats the exclusivity element (Tran v. Macha).
Landlocked road access must proceed under easement by necessity, not prior use (Hamrick v. Ward).
A specific metes-and-bounds description controls over a general description when they cannot be harmonized (Concho v. Ellison).

content.html:
Why case law matters to retracement

Statutes and the dignity-of-calls hierarchy supply the framework, but Texas case law supplies the controlling holdings a court will actually apply. Advanced analysis means briefing each leading case for its precise rule and then applying that rule to new facts — exactly what an expert surveyor does when forming a boundary opinion. This week consolidates the cases threaded through the course: the footsteps and dignity-of-calls foundation, the easement and prescription rules, the water-boundary line, and the modern deed-construction canon. The goal is to predict how a Texas court would locate the boundary, not merely to recite the holdings.

sections.title: Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006) — prescriptive easements disfavored
sections.content: Neighbors shared a driveway under a mutual mistake about the boundary. The Texas Supreme Court reversed the easement, holding that joint or shared use does not satisfy the exclusivity element of a prescriptive easement: 'joint use of a road, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement.' Because most road-sharing fact patterns involve some owner use, Texas prescriptive claims rarely succeed, and access disputes are funneled toward necessity easements and express grants.

sections.title: Hamrick v. Ward, 446 S.W.3d 377 (Tex. 2014) — access via necessity, not prior use
sections.content: For roadway-access claims to a landlocked, previously unified parcel, the claimant must proceed under easement by necessity, not implied easement by prior use. This narrowed a long-unsettled doctrine and channels landlocked road access into the stricter necessity framework (unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity at severance). Prior use still applies to non-access uses such as utility lines where it remains 'reasonably necessary.'

sections.title: Severance v. Patterson, 370 S.W.3d 705 (Tex. 2012) — limits on the rolling easement
sections.content: A public beach easement under the Open Beaches Act may migrate gradually with a naturally shifting beach, but it does not automatically 'roll' onto previously private dry land after an avulsive event such as a hurricane. After avulsion the State must establish a new easement. The case imports the accretion/avulsion distinction into beach-access law.

sections.title: Concho Resources v. Ellison (Tex. 2021) — specific controls general
sections.content: A modern boundary/deed-conflict decision emphasizing the intent expressed in the instrument and holding that a specific metes-and-bounds description controls over a general description when the two cannot be harmonized. Reinforces the four-corners, harmonize-the-whole approach of Bass v. Harper.

rows: Dignity of calls; follow the original surveyor's footsteps
Landlocked road access via necessity, not prior use
Unity of ownership required for easement by necessity
MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants, MHW for Anglo grants
No rolling beach easement after avulsion

content.text: Texas note: the most heavily tested 'Texas-differs' holdings are the prescriptive-easement exclusivity rule (Tran v. Macha) and the necessity-over-prior-use rule for landlocked access (Hamrick v. Ward). Both make Texas access rights harder to acquire by implication than general common law.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Brief each case for its precise rule, then apply the rule to new facts.
Tran v. Macha: joint use defeats prescriptive exclusivity; Hamrick: landlocked access via necessity.
Severance: no rolling beach easement after avulsion; Concho: specific controls general.
Synthesize cases with statutes to predict how a court would locate the boundary.

flashcards.definition: Texas Supreme Court case holding that joint or shared use cannot satisfy the exclusivity element of a prescriptive easement; 'joint use, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement.'
flashcards.hint_1: Shared driveway defeats prescription.

flashcards.definition: Texas Supreme Court case requiring that roadway-access claims to a landlocked, previously unified parcel proceed under easement by necessity rather than implied easement by prior use.
flashcards.hint_1: Landlocked access = necessity, not prior use.

flashcards.definition: Modern Texas deed-conflict decision holding that a specific metes-and-bounds description controls over a general description when the two cannot be harmonized, consistent with four-corners intent.
flashcards.hint_1: Specific calls beat a general recital.

lessons.title: Week 13: Writing Boundary Opinions and Expert Reports
learning_objectives: Structure a defensible boundary opinion that states evidence, analysis, and conclusion.
Draft and defend a metes-and-bounds legal description that closes and ties to the original survey.
Describe the surveyor's role and duties as an expert witness in boundary litigation.
key_takeaways: A boundary opinion marshals the evidence, applies the controlling Texas doctrine, and states a reasoned conclusion the surveyor can defend.
A defensible legal description is definite, unambiguous, closed, monumented, and tied to the original GLO survey and a stated basis of bearing.
An expert surveyor's opinions must rest on a reliable methodology (footsteps, dignity of calls, record analysis) and be explainable on cross-examination.

content.html:
From measurements to a defensible opinion

A boundary opinion is the surveyor's reasoned conclusion about where a line is, supported so it can withstand scrutiny. Its structure mirrors how a court reasons: state the evidence (record instruments, found monuments, occupation, testimony, GLO records); state the analysis (intent of the controlling instrument, dignity of calls, footsteps, senior/junior rights, applicable statutes and cases); and state the conclusion with the reasoning that connects evidence to result. The opinion should explain not only the line adopted but why competing positions were rejected. Because boundary location is ultimately a question of fact, a well-reasoned opinion grounded in Texas doctrine is the surveyor's most valuable product.

Writing and defending the legal description

A defensible metes-and-bounds description satisfies 22 TAC §138.91: it provides a definite and unambiguous identification of the boundaries, describes all monuments found or placed, and references courses to a stated basis of bearing. Per Texas convention it carries a caption citing the original GLO survey and abstract, ties the point of beginning to a corner of that original survey, runs a continuous series of bearing-and-distance calls with monument ties, and closes mathematically back to the point of beginning. State which monuments were found and which were placed, and recite controlling monuments of record dignity relied upon. A description that does not close, or that cannot be relocated independently of the surveyor's own work, is legally defective and indefensible.

rows: County, original GLO survey + abstract, parent-tract reference
Tied to a corner of the original survey or a found monument
Continuous bearing + distance with monument ties
Stated datum (e.g., Texas State Plane, NAD83)
Found vs. placed identified; record-dignity monuments recited
Returns to the POB; closed figure with no gap/overlap

content.text: Defensibility test: could a different competent surveyor relocate this boundary from the description and record alone, without your field notes? If not, the description is not yet definite and unambiguous under 22 TAC 138.91.

content.html:
The surveyor as expert witness

In boundary litigation the surveyor often testifies as an expert. Expert opinions must rest on a reliable methodology — the recognized retracement framework of following the original surveyor's footsteps, weighing monuments by dignity of calls, analyzing the record chain and senior/junior rights, and applying the controlling statutes and cases — and must be explainable in plain terms on direct and cross-examination. The expert should disclose assumptions, the evidence relied upon and rejected, and the limits of the opinion. Credibility flows from transparent methodology and consistency with the record; an opinion that cannot be traced back to evidence and accepted doctrine will not persuade a factfinder.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A boundary opinion states evidence, analysis (intent, dignity of calls, footsteps), and a reasoned conclusion.
A defensible description is definite, unambiguous, closed, monumented, and tied to the original GLO survey (22 TAC 138.91).
Test defensibility by whether another surveyor could relocate the line from the description and record alone.
Expert testimony must rest on reliable retracement methodology and be explainable on cross-examination.

flashcards.definition: A surveyor's reasoned, defensible conclusion about a line's location, structured as evidence, analysis under controlling Texas doctrine, and conclusion that explains why competing positions were rejected.
flashcards.hint_1: Evidence + analysis + reasoned conclusion.

flashcards.definition: The 22 TAC 138.91 standard: a description that unmistakably identifies the boundaries, states found vs. placed monuments and a basis of bearing, closes, and can be relocated independently of the surveyor's own notes.
flashcards.hint_1: Another surveyor could relocate it cold.

flashcards.definition: The accepted retracement basis for a surveyor's expert opinion: follow the footsteps, weigh monuments by dignity of calls, analyze the record chain and senior/junior rights, apply controlling statutes and cases.
flashcards.hint_1: Footsteps, dignity of calls, record analysis.

lessons.title: Week 14: SIT/RPLS Exam Preparation — Legal Principles
learning_objectives: Review the legal-principles topics most heavily tested on the SIT/RPLS examinations.
Apply rapid issue-spotting to scenario questions on boundaries, easements, and water law.
Distinguish the Texas-specific rules examiners use to separate strong candidates.
key_takeaways: Exam success rests on knowing the controlling rule and the Texas-specific exceptions, not on memorizing case facts.
High-yield Texas topics: dignity of calls/footsteps, senior/junior vs. apportionment, gradient boundary, Luttes datum, prescriptive-easement exclusivity, adverse-possession tracks.
Read scenario questions for the trigger fact that selects the doctrine.

content.html:
How the legal-principles exam thinks

The legal-principles portions of the SIT (Fundamentals) and RPLS (Texas-specific) examinations reward applying rules to scenarios. Pure definition recall is necessary but not sufficient; most questions embed a fact pattern and ask which doctrine controls or what result follows. The fastest path is disciplined issue-spotting: find the trigger fact (creation order, a found monument, a 30-foot navigable stream, a Spanish/Mexican grant, joint driveway use, a quitclaim) and map it to the controlling rule. This week drills the highest-yield Texas topics and the exceptions examiners use to separate candidates who merely memorized from those who understand.

rows: Natural > artificial > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity; yields to intent/footsteps
Sequential = senior/junior; simultaneous = apportion by record proportion
Obliterated re-established from evidence; only lost corners are proportioned
30-ft average bed width; gradient boundary; State owns the bed
MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants; MHW for Anglo/Texas grants (Luttes)
Exclusivity required; joint use is fatal (Tran v. Macha)
Texas is a pure notice state (Prop. Code 13.001-13.002)

content.text: Texas exam traps: adverse possession is in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code Ch. 16, NOT the Property Code; the surveying standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138, NOT the repealed Ch. 663; and the navigable-stream test is a 30-foot average bed width, not a commerce test.

sections.title: Adverse-possession tracks (CPRC 16.024-16.028)
sections.content: 3-year (16.024): title or color of title. 5-year (16.025): cultivate/use + pay taxes + duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed; a single missed tax year is fatal. 10-year (16.026): cultivate/use, no instrument or taxes needed, capped at 160 acres absent enclosure or a recorded deed fixing boundaries. 25-year (16.027): runs regardless of legal disability. 25-year (16.028): under a recorded instrument even if void on its face (excludes quitclaim/forged).

sections.title: Easement quick rules
sections.content: Easement by necessity requires unity of ownership before severance, strict (not mere convenience) necessity, and necessity existing at severance; it terminates when the necessity ends (Koonce; Staley). Landlocked road access goes through necessity, not prior use (Hamrick). Prescriptive easements are disfavored and require exclusivity; joint use is fatal (Tran v. Macha).

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Spot the trigger fact, then map it to the controlling rule.
Memorize the Texas-specific exceptions: gradient boundary, Luttes datum, prescriptive exclusivity, adverse-possession tracks.
Avoid the classic traps: AP is in CPRC Ch. 16; standards are in 22 TAC Ch. 138; navigability is a 30-foot bed-width test.
Texas is a pure notice recording state.

flashcards.definition: Texas's statutory limitation periods in CPRC 16.024-16.028: 3-yr (title/color), 5-yr (taxes + registered deed), 10-yr (160-acre cap), and two 25-yr tracks (disability long-stop; void recorded instrument).
flashcards.hint_1: 3/5/10/25 each with its own elements.

flashcards.definition: Texas's recording regime (Prop. Code 13.001-13.002): a subsequent purchaser for value without notice takes free of a prior unrecorded conveyance and prevails even if the BFP records later or never records.
flashcards.hint_1: BFP without notice wins, no race needed.

lessons.title: Week 15: Comprehensive Review and Integration
learning_objectives: Integrate the full course into a single retracement decision sequence.
Apply the framework end-to-end to a multi-issue boundary scenario.
Connect Texas-specific doctrines to the surveyor's professional standards and liability.
key_takeaways: The retracement sequence runs: intent, footsteps, found monuments by dignity of calls, senior/junior, then apportionment as a last resort.
Texas-specific overlays (GLO origin, gradient boundary, Luttes datum, severed minerals, dedication limits) reshape the general framework.
Professional standards (22 TAC Ch. 138) and a defensible opinion tie the analysis to practice and liability.

content.html:
One decision sequence for retracement

The entire course collapses into a single ordered method the surveyor applies to any boundary problem. (1) Intent: construe the controlling instrument from its four corners for the parties' intent. (2) Footsteps: locate the original surveyor's actual lines and corners; if ascertainable, they control. (3) Monuments by dignity of calls: weigh found natural and artificial monuments over course, distance, and quantity. (4) Senior/junior rights: resolve overlaps in sequential conveyances in the senior's favor. (5) Apportionment: as a genuine last resort, proportion excess/deficiency among simultaneously created lots, but only for lost corners after found monuments and footsteps fail. Throughout, isolate gross blunders and never proportion them.

rows: Chain of title to original grant
Chain runs to a sovereign grant via GLO survey + abstract (no PLSS)
Gradient boundary on navigable streams (Motl v. Boyd)
Dual datum: MHHW for Spanish/Mexican grants (Luttes)
Disfavored; joint use fatal (Tran v. Macha)
Abolished in counties under 50,000 since 1981 (Transp. Code 281)
Mineral estate often severed and dominant; Small Bill reserves streambed minerals

content.html:
Working a multi-issue scenario

Apply the sequence to a composite: a 1900 deed carves a tract from a larger holding within the John Doe Survey, Abstract No. 123; the deed calls to a creek and to an iron stake now found; a 1925 plat later subdivides an adjoining tract; the boundary fronts a river that averages 35 feet of bed width; and the original grant is a State patent. The surveyor: construes the 1900 deed for intent; follows the original footsteps and honors the found creek (natural monument) and iron stake (artificial monument) over course and distance; treats the 1900 carve-out as senior to the 1925 plat (sequential), so any overlap favors the senior tract while the plat lots among themselves are apportioned (simultaneous); locates the river boundary by the gradient boundary because 35 feet exceeds the 30-foot navigability threshold; and, the grant being a State patent, applies the mean-high-tide family of rules rather than MHHW if any coastal frontage existed. Each step is documented in the opinion.

content.text: Integration check: for every conclusion, the surveyor should be able to name the controlling authority (Stafford, Motl v. Boyd, Luttes, Tran v. Macha, 212.004, CPRC Ch. 16) and the 22 TAC Ch. 138 standard the work product satisfies.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Apply the sequence: intent, footsteps, monuments by dignity, senior/junior, apportionment last.
Layer the Texas-specific overlays onto the general framework at each step.
Isolate gross blunders; never proportion them.
Tie every conclusion to a named authority and to the 22 TAC Ch. 138 standard met.

lessons.title: Week 16: Final Exam
learning_objectives: Demonstrate integrated command of advanced Texas boundary law across the course.
Resolve multi-issue scenarios with the correct doctrine and authority.
Produce defensible reasoning connecting evidence, doctrine, and conclusion.
key_takeaways: The final is cumulative; expect multi-issue scenarios spanning conveyances, water, minerals, condemnation, platting, and case law.
Credit follows correct doctrine selection and authority citation as much as computation.
Defensible reasoning links evidence to controlling Texas rule to conclusion.

content.html:
Cumulative scope

The final examination is comprehensive, drawing on every week: advanced deed analysis; simultaneous versus sequential conveyances and apportionment; overlaps, gaps, vacancies, and proportionate measurement; complex riparian and littoral boundaries; the severed and dominant mineral estate; eminent domain and condemnation surveys; subdivision platting and dedication; ALTA/NSPS Land Title Surveys and Table A; the TBPELS rules in 22 TAC Ch. 138; advanced Texas case law; and writing defensible boundary opinions. Expect multi-issue scenarios that require you to identify several doctrines in one fact pattern and resolve them in the right order.

content.text: Final-exam discipline: for each scenario, (1) name the controlling doctrine, (2) cite the Texas authority, (3) apply it to the facts, (4) state the conclusion. This structure earns credit even where a computed value is imperfect.

content.html:
Demonstrating defensible reasoning

The strongest answers read like a compact boundary opinion: they marshal the relevant evidence, apply the controlling Texas doctrine by name, explain why competing resolutions fail, and state a clear conclusion. Watch for the recurring 'Texas-differs' hooks — the gradient boundary on navigable streams, the Luttes dual coastal datum, the disfavored prescriptive easement (Tran v. Macha), the necessity-over-prior-use rule (Hamrick v. Ward), the adverse-possession tracks in CPRC Ch. 16, and the current surveying standards in 22 TAC Ch. 138 (not the repealed Ch. 663). Demonstrating that you can move from evidence to controlling rule to a defensible conclusion is the entire objective of Legal Principles II.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: The final is cumulative and scenario-based across all course topics.
Structure each answer: doctrine, authority, application, conclusion.
Lead with the Texas-specific rules examiners emphasize.
Defensible reasoning connecting evidence to rule to conclusion is the goal.
