quiz_questions.question_text: A patent's metes-and-bounds calls conflict with a found, identified original monument on the ground. Under Texas law, which controls?
options: The found, identified original monument
The stated quantity/acreage
The course (bearing) call
The distance call
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The found, identified original monument
quiz_questions.explanation: Per Stafford v. King and the dignity-of-calls hierarchy, a found and identified original monument outranks conflicting course, distance, and quantity calls; the hierarchy yields to the footsteps of the original surveyor. Quantity is the weakest call.

quiz_questions.question_text: The dignity-of-calls hierarchy is a rigid, mechanical formula that always controls over the parties' intent.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The hierarchy is only a rule of construction (a set of reliability presumptions). It always yields to the controlling intent of the instrument and to the original surveyor's footsteps.

quiz_questions.question_text: A developer records a plat of 30 lots. A resurvey finds the block measures longer than the record. How is the excess handled among the lots?
options: Apportioned among the lots in proportion to their record lengths
Given entirely to the first lot sold
Given entirely to the last lot sold
Given to the senior lot by recording date
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Apportioned among the lots in proportion to their record lengths
quiz_questions.explanation: Platted lots are simultaneous conveyances of equal dignity, so no lot is senior. Excess or deficiency is apportioned proportionately by record length; the order lots later sold is immaterial.

quiz_questions.question_text: A grantor sells Tract A in 1990, then Tract B (adjoining) in 1998 from the same parent tract, and their descriptions overlap. Who prevails in the overlap?
options: Tract A, the senior conveyance, takes the overlap
The overlap is split equally between A and B
Tract B, the junior conveyance, takes the overlap
The overlap reverts to the grantor
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Tract A, the senior conveyance, takes the overlap
quiz_questions.explanation: These are sequential conveyances governed by senior/junior rights. The senior (1990) grant takes its full description; the junior (1998) is cut back to the remainder. A grantor cannot convey land already conveyed.

quiz_questions.question_text: An original corner is gone, but its position can be recovered from an old fence built to it and references in an adjoining deed. How should it be re-established?
options: From the collateral evidence; it is obliterated, not lost, and must not be proportioned
By single proportionate measurement
By double proportionate measurement
By splitting the difference between adjoining lots
quiz_questions.correct_answer: From the collateral evidence; it is obliterated, not lost, and must not be proportioned
quiz_questions.explanation: A corner whose position is recoverable from collateral evidence is obliterated, not lost. It is re-established from that evidence. Only a genuinely lost corner (no recoverable evidence) is restored by proportionate measurement.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boundary follows a navigable Texas stream. Under Motl v. Boyd, where is the line dividing State-owned bed from private upland?
options: The gradient boundary, midway up the qualified cut bank
The thread/center of the channel
The ordinary high-water mark
The line of mean higher high water
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The gradient boundary, midway up the qualified cut bank
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas uses the gradient boundary (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard) — a graded line midway between the water level that just reaches the cut bank and the level that just fails to overtop it. The center-thread rule applies to non-navigable streams.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal tract traces to an 1829 Mexican grant. Under Luttes v. State, what is the seaward boundary datum?
options: Mean higher high water (MHHW)
Mean high tide / mean high water (MHW)
Mean low tide
The vegetation line
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Mean higher high water (MHHW)
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Luttes, Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by mean higher high water, which lies landward; Anglo-American/Texas grants use mean high tide. The sovereign source selects the datum.

quiz_questions.question_text: In Texas, which estate is dominant when the surface and mineral estates have been severed?
options: The mineral estate, with an implied right of reasonable surface use
The surface estate, which can exclude mineral development
Neither; they are perfectly co-equal in all respects
Whichever was conveyed later in time
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The mineral estate, with an implied right of reasonable surface use
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas treats the mineral estate as dominant, carrying an implied right to make reasonable use of the surface to develop minerals, tempered by the accommodation doctrine. The surface estate is servient to that right.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pipeline condemns a 50-foot strip across a 100-acre farm. What valuation framework applies to the partial taking?
options: Parent-tract analysis: value of the part taken plus damages to the remainder, offset by special benefits
Market value of the entire 100 acres
The strip's value only, with no remainder analysis
A flat statutory per-acre rate
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Parent-tract analysis: value of the part taken plus damages to the remainder, offset by special benefits
quiz_questions.explanation: A partial take is valued by a parent-tract analysis: the value of the part taken plus severance damages to the remainder, offset by any special benefits. The whole-take measure (entire market value) applies only when the entire parcel is taken.

quiz_questions.question_text: An owner divides a tract within a city's ETJ into subdivision lots using metes-and-bounds descriptions in deeds, recording no plat. Is this permissible under Local Gov't Code 212.004?
options: No; a plat is required, and 212.004 expressly includes divisions by metes-and-bounds
Yes; metes-and-bounds deeds are exempt from platting
Yes; the ETJ has no platting authority
Only if each lot exceeds one acre
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No; a plat is required, and 212.004 expressly includes divisions by metes-and-bounds
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 212.004 requires a plat for subdividing within a municipality or its ETJ and expressly states that a division 'includes a division made by using a metes and bounds description,' closing the deed loophole.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a Texas county of 40,000 population, a road has been used by the public for decades but was never dedicated in writing. Since 1981, can implied dedication establish it as a public road?
options: No; Transp. Code Ch. 281 abolished implied dedication in counties of 50,000 or fewer
Yes; long public use always creates an implied dedication
Yes; implied dedication is unaffected by statute
Only if the county graded the road once
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No; Transp. Code Ch. 281 abolished implied dedication in counties of 50,000 or fewer
quiz_questions.explanation: Since August 1981, in counties of 50,000 or fewer, a public road interest can be acquired only by written dedication, purchase, condemnation, or an adverse-possession judgment. 'Everyone has always used it' is insufficient.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of an ALTA/NSPS Land Title Survey?
options: To serve a title insurer and lender in a commercial transaction by plotting record matters against the ground
To set permanent monuments only
To establish a new subdivision plat
To determine mineral ownership
quiz_questions.correct_answer: To serve a title insurer and lender in a commercial transaction by plotting record matters against the ground
quiz_questions.explanation: An ALTA/NSPS survey serves the title company and lender, letting the insurer address the survey exception. It plots Schedule B easements, locates improvements and encroachments, and certifies to named parties.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the national ALTA/NSPS standard relate to the Texas Category 1A survey?
options: Texas surveyors often perform one survey to satisfy both; Category 1A is defined via the TSPS Manual incorporated by the TBPELS rules
Category 1A replaced the ALTA/NSPS standard in Texas
They are identical and interchangeable in all respects
Category 1A is a federal standard
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Texas surveyors often perform one survey to satisfy both; Category 1A is defined via the TSPS Manual incorporated by the TBPELS rules
quiz_questions.explanation: Category 1A is Texas's highest land-title standard, defined by the TSPS Manual of Practice incorporated by reference into the TBPELS rules. Surveyors commonly perform one survey meeting both standards; Category 1A requires a separate closure report.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best describes ALTA/NSPS Table A items?
options: Optional, negotiated additional services selected in advance by the client, lender, and title company
Mandatory items required on every ALTA survey
A list of title exceptions the surveyor must remove
Federal flood-insurance requirements
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Optional, negotiated additional services selected in advance by the client, lender, and title company
quiz_questions.explanation: Table A is a menu of optional items (e.g., monuments set, area, flood zone, zoning, utilities) selected and negotiated before fieldwork. Each selected item expands scope, fee, and certification responsibility.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where are the current TBPELS technical standards of practice for surveyors located?
options: 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E
22 TAC Ch. 663
The Texas Property Code
The federal BLM Manual
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E
quiz_questions.explanation: After HB 1523 (2019) merged the boards into TBPELS, the standards were re-codified into 22 TAC Ch. 138, Subchapter E (sections 138.81 et seq.). The former Chs. 661/663 were repealed; citing Ch. 663 in 2026 is outdated.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two neighbors shared a driveway for over twenty years under a mutual mistake about the boundary. Under Tran v. Macha, can the user claim a prescriptive easement?
options: No; joint use defeats the exclusivity element and cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement
Yes; twenty years of use always creates a prescriptive easement
Yes; mutual mistake satisfies hostility
Only if the user paid taxes on the strip
quiz_questions.correct_answer: No; joint use defeats the exclusivity element and cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement
quiz_questions.explanation: Tran v. Macha holds that joint or shared use does not satisfy exclusivity; 'joint use, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement.' Texas disfavors prescriptive easements and scrutinizes every element.

quiz_questions.question_text: A landowner whose previously unified parcel is now landlocked seeks an easement for road access. Under Hamrick v. Ward, which doctrine must the claim proceed under?
options: Easement by necessity
Implied easement by prior use
Prescriptive easement
Express easement
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Easement by necessity
quiz_questions.explanation: Hamrick v. Ward channels roadway-access claims to a landlocked, previously unified parcel into easement by necessity rather than prior use. Prior use still applies to non-access uses like utility lines where reasonably necessary.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is the best test of whether a metes-and-bounds legal description is 'definite and unambiguous' under 22 TAC 138.91?
options: Another competent surveyor could relocate the boundary from the description and record alone
It uses the maximum number of decimal places
It cites the most recent case law
It omits any reference to monuments
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Another competent surveyor could relocate the boundary from the description and record alone
quiz_questions.explanation: A defensible description must let another surveyor relocate the line independently of the author's field notes. It must close, state found vs. placed monuments and a basis of bearing, and tie to the original survey.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which Texas code are the adverse-possession limitation periods located?
options: Civil Practice & Remedies Code Ch. 16
Property Code
Natural Resources Code Ch. 21
Occupations Code Ch. 1071
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Civil Practice & Remedies Code Ch. 16
quiz_questions.explanation: Adverse possession is in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code Ch. 16, sections 16.024-16.028 (a common trap), not the Property Code. The five-year track additionally requires paying taxes and a duly registered deed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas is a pure 'notice' recording state, so a bona fide purchaser for value without notice prevails over a prior unrecorded grantee even if the BFP records second or never records.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Under Prop. Code 13.001-13.002, a subsequent purchaser for value without notice takes free of a prior unrecorded conveyance and wins regardless of recording order; there is no race requirement for the BFP.

quiz_questions.question_text: Place the retracement decision sequence in the correct order of priority.
options: Intent, footsteps, found monuments by dignity of calls, senior/junior rights, apportionment last
Apportionment first, then monuments, then intent
Quantity, distance, course, monuments
Senior/junior rights before intent and footsteps
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Intent, footsteps, found monuments by dignity of calls, senior/junior rights, apportionment last
quiz_questions.explanation: The ordered method is: construe for intent, follow the original surveyor's footsteps, weigh found monuments by dignity of calls, apply senior/junior rights to overlaps, and proportion only as a last resort for lost corners.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boundary fronts a river averaging 35 feet of bed width, and the parcel's original grant is a State of Texas patent. Which two rules apply to locating the water boundary and (if coastal) the tidal datum?
options: Gradient boundary (navigable, >30 ft) and, for an Anglo/Texas grant, mean high tide
Center-thread rule and mean higher high water
Ordinary high-water mark and the vegetation line
Gradient boundary and mean higher high water
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Gradient boundary (navigable, >30 ft) and, for an Anglo/Texas grant, mean high tide
quiz_questions.explanation: At 35 feet the stream exceeds the 30-foot navigability threshold, so the gradient boundary applies. The grant is a State (Anglo) patent, so any coastal frontage uses mean high tide, not the MHHW reserved for Spanish/Mexican grants.

quiz_questions.question_text: On the final exam, citing the controlling Texas authority by name alongside a correct conclusion is unnecessary as long as the arithmetic is perfect.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: Credit follows correct doctrine selection and authority citation as much as computation. The strongest answers name the controlling rule and case, apply it to the facts, and state a defensible conclusion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Texas adverse possession's 5-year track (CPRC 16.025) requires the claimant to pay the applicable property taxes and hold under a duly registered, non-quitclaim deed.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: The 5-year track (16.025) requires cultivating/using the land, paying applicable taxes each year (a single missed year is fatal), and claiming under a duly registered deed that excludes quitclaim and forged deeds.

problem_templates.name: Single proportionate measurement — corrected interior distance
problem_templates.question_template: A block of {{n}} lots was platted, each with a record frontage of {{rec:f2}} ft, giving a record block length of {{recTotal:f2}} ft between two found, undisputed original corner monuments. A resurvey finds the actual measured length between those same monuments is {{meas:f2}} ft. Using single proportionate measurement, what is the corrected frontage (ft) of each lot?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Lots on a recorded plat are simultaneous conveyances of equal dignity, so the measured excess/deficiency is apportioned proportionately. Each record-equal lot becomes the measured length divided by the number of lots: {{meas:f2}} ÷ {{n}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Gap or overlap width between adjoining record calls
problem_templates.question_template: Surveyed from a common found corner along the same line, a senior deed calls a distance of {{senior:f2}} ft to its boundary, while the adjoining junior deed's described limit falls at {{junior:f2}} ft from that corner. What is the width (ft) of the strip in conflict (a positive value indicates a gap/hiatus; the same magnitude would be an overlap if the calls ran toward each other)?

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the two record limits
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Senior limit = {{senior:f2}} ft; junior limit = {{junior:f2}} ft

problem_templates.explanation_template: The width of the conflict strip is the absolute difference between the two record limits along the common line: |{{junior:f2}} − {{senior:f2}}| = {{_answer}} ft. Whether it is a gap (claimed by neither) or an overlap (claimed by both) depends on the direction the calls run; resolution still follows senior/junior rights and the original footsteps.
